sections.heading: Configure a Stream Processing Workspace
sections.body:
To get started with Atlas Stream Processing, you must first [Stream Processing Workspaces](atlas-sp-manage-spi).
This involves learning how to create, modify, and delete an Atlas Stream Processing Workspace to begin processing
your streaming data.

sections.heading: Streaming Data
sections.body:
A stream is a continuous flow of immutable data originating from one
or more sources. Examples of data streams include temperature or
pressure readings from sensors, records of financial transactions, or
change data capture events.

Data streams originate from **sources** such as [Apache Kafka Topics](https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#intro_concepts_and_terms/) or
MongoDB [change streams](manual/changeStreams). You can then write
processed data to **sinks** including [Apache Kafka Topics](https://kafka.apache.org/documentation/#intro_concepts_and_terms/), Atlas
collections, external functions, or cloud data stores.

Atlas Stream Processing provides native stream processing capabilities to operate
on continuous data without the time and computational constraints of
an at-rest database.

sections.heading: Structure of a Stream Processor
sections.body:
[Stream processors](atlas-sp-manage-processor) take the form of a pipeline which can be
conceptually divided into three phases. Once you understand this structure, you can create and manage stream processors
to continuously process your streaming data.

sections.heading: Sources
sections.body:
Stream processors begin by ingesting documents from sources of
streaming data to which Atlas Stream Processing is connected. These can be
broker systems like [Apache Kafka](https://kafka.apache.org/), or database change streams such as
those generated by Atlas read/write operations. These inputs
must be valid `json` or `ejson` documents. Once the
[$source](pipe.$source) stage ingests a document, you can apply
[MongoDB aggregation](manual/aggregation) to that document
to transform it as needed.

In addition to ingesting data from a streaming source, Atlas Stream Processing
also supports enriching your documents with data from [HTTPS](atlas-sp-agg-https) requests and [$lookup](atlas-sp-agg-lookup) operations to join data from connected
Atlas clusters.

sections.heading: Pipelines
sections.body:
A stream processor leverages [Aggregation pipeline stages](atlas-sp-aggregation) and [Aggregation operators](atlas-sp-aggregation-operators) in addition to the standard MongoDB
suite of [aggregation](manual/aggregation) to transform
ingested data and extract valuable insights. To learn how to define Atlas Stream Processing
[aggregation pipelines](atlas-sp-agg-source), see the aggregation
pipeline documentation.

Atlas Stream Processing can write documents it can't process
to a [Dead Letter Queue](atlas-sp-dlq).

You can enrich documents by restructuring them, adding or removing
fields, looking up information from your collections, and
more. Atlas Stream Processing also enables you to collect events using
[windows](atlas-sp-processor-structure-window) and execute
arbitrary [functions](atlas-sp-processor-structure-function).

sections.heading: Windows
sections.body:
[Stream Processor Windows](atlas-sp-windows) are pipeline stages that aggregate
streaming data within a set time period. This enables you to group the
data, take averages, find minima and maxima, and perform various other
operations that are otherwise inapplicable to streaming data. Each
stream processor can only have one window stage.

sections.heading: Functions
sections.body:
Atlas Stream Processing supports calls to either custom JavaScript
[functions](atlas-sp-agg-function) or AWS (Amazon Web Services) Lambda
[functions](atlas-sp-agg-external-function) that run against
each document that the stream processes passes to them.

sections.heading: Sinks
sections.body:
After processing the ingested data, the stream processor persits it be
writing it to a sink. Atlas Stream Processing offers the [$emit](atlas-sp-agg-emit) and [$merge](atlas-sp-agg-merge) stages for
writing to different sink types. These stages are mutually exclusive
with each other, and each stream processor can have only one sink
stage. Your pipeline can include logic to write processed documents to
different Kafka topics or Atlas collections within the same sink
connections.

sections.heading: Atlas Stream Processing Regions
sections.body:
Atlas Stream Processing supports creating stream processing workspaces on AWS (Amazon Web Services), Azure (Microsoft Azure), and Google Cloud. For a
list of available regions, see the Stream Processing Worspaces
sections of the:

- [Amazon Web Services](aws-stream-processing-regions)
  feature reference.

- [Microsoft Azure](azure-stream-processing-regions)
  feature reference.

- [Google Cloud Platform](gcp-stream-processing-regions)
  feature reference.

Stream processors can read from and write to clusters hosted on
different cloud providers, or in different regions.

sections.heading: Billing
sections.body: For information on billing, see the [Atlas Stream Processing](stream-processing-costs) billing page.

sections.heading: Next Steps
sections.body:
To begin working hands-on with Atlas Stream Processing, see
[Get Started with Atlas Stream Processing](atlas-sp-quickstart).

For more detailed information on core Atlas Stream Processing concepts,
see the following:

- [Stream Processor Windows](atlas-sp-windows)

- [Atlas Stream Processing Access and Auditing](atlas-sp-security)

Learn about specific [limitations](atlas-sp-limitations) of Atlas Stream Processing.
